title: Borderline Personalities

jokes.text: So hot. Anybody hallucinating yet? Did anybody else hear that iguana speak? Let's kill it.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is running a spiritual sweat lodge for CEOs
jokes.punchline: The 'enlightenment' immediately devolves into hallucinations and murder plots
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation from spiritual seeking to homicidal hallucinations. Economical progression that captures the absurdity of corporate wellness culture.

jokes.text: I'm not an iguana. It's part of the process. They're ready. They're ready!
jokes.setup: CEOs are trying to kill George Sr. thinking he's an iguana
jokes.punchline: George Sr. calmly treats attempted murder as a positive sign of progress
jokes.explanation: Perfect George Sr. response - turning near-death into a business opportunity. The deadpan 'they're ready' is particularly strong.

jokes.text: I got you. You know, guys, even I, Father B., when I first started these sweat lodges, I would come out of there thinking, oh, God, I wish I had a... a lemonade. Just like a child, you know. Cold water, some lemons, a little sugar, you know. That's what I gave my power to!
jokes.setup: George Sr. is giving a spiritual lesson about overcoming weakness
jokes.punchline: His example of profound spiritual weakness is wanting lemonade
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy showing George Sr.'s shallow interpretation of spiritual struggle. Setup is a bit long but payoff works.

jokes.text: She is silent, but in that silence, there is strength. She needs no words to communicate to you. Boundless power to you, too, Heartfire.
jokes.setup: George Sr. introduces Heartfire as a mystical silent aura specialist
jokes.punchline: He immediately tries to have a conversation with her despite emphasizing her silence
jokes.explanation: Good contradiction humor - George Sr. immediately violates his own spiritual principle. Well-structured but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Hell, I was so weak, you guys, I was willing to spend $10,000 just a little... sip of this lemonade. Fifteen! But now I can spend two hours in brutal heat with a cup of this cold, refreshing lemonade and just pour it in the dirt.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is demonstrating his spiritual growth and power over material desires
jokes.punchline: His demonstration of power is wastefully pouring out expensive lemonade while others desperately want it
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic perfectly suited to George Sr. The escalation to $15k and then wasting it is beautifully constructed cruelty.

jokes.text: Ah, come on, Daniels! You ran Bear Stearns, for God's sake! I was like this. Just like this.
jokes.setup: A CEO is suffering in the heat
jokes.punchline: George Sr. shames him by referencing his professional credentials while demonstrating weakness
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy with Bear Stearns reference (financial crisis topical humor). The visual of George Sr. demonstrating weakness while lecturing is strong.

jokes.text: How was the party? Mom stole it. The whole boat? She had.
jokes.setup: George Michael reports on the Queen Mary party
jokes.punchline: George Sr. misunderstands 'stole the show' literally as stealing the boat
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection joke with good economy. Not particularly original but well-executed setup/payoff structure.

jokes.text: I'm wondering if we shouldn't have a plan in regards to bookkeeping, in case we accidentally make a mistake or get caught. What if we were to become a... an-an... an NLC? A 'No Liability Corporation.' We just have no liability, just nothing...
jokes.setup: Barry is trying to suggest a legal business structure
jokes.punchline: He invents a nonsensical 'No Liability Corporation' that doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barry Zuckerkorn incompetence - making up legal terms that sound official but are completely wrong. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Take to the sea! Three miles out, and it's a free-for-all! No rules, pirate radio laws. Really! Is this true, Mr. Zuckerkorn? Sure.
jokes.setup: Barry is giving legal advice about maritime law
jokes.punchline: His confident 'Sure' confirms completely made-up legal advice
jokes.explanation: Classic Barry moment - confidently confirming nonsense legal advice. 'Take to the sea' becomes a running gag. Good escalation and quotable.

jokes.text: you can't try a husband and wife for the same crime, right? Sure. We have the best attorneys.
jokes.setup: Someone asks Barry about spousal immunity law
jokes.punchline: Barry's 'Sure' confirms another legal misconception, followed by ironic praise of their legal team
jokes.explanation: Continuing the Barry incompetence pattern. The ironic 'best attorneys' line works well as immediate contradiction of the evidence.

jokes.text: She's fine. You're lucky we didn't have an autumn, otherwise she'd be colder. Look what they've done, George! Look what the homosexuals have done to me. A joyride. That's what they decide to do with the freedom this country has given them.
jokes.setup: Lucille is explaining her situation after being arrested
jokes.punchline: She blames 'the homosexuals' for taking a joyride with the Queen Mary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille - blaming a random minority group for her own criminal actions with complete conviction. Strong character voice and absurd logic.

jokes.text: First of all, won't be cheap. It's gonna cost you a fortune in legal fees. What's the good news? Oh, yeah. Well, from your perspective, I can see where you think it's all bad news. It's all bad news.
jokes.setup: Barry says it's a 'good news/bad news' situation and starts with the bad news
jokes.punchline: He realizes there actually is no good news and admits it's all bad
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection structure - sets up expectation of balance then reveals there's no good news. Perfect Barry incompetence and confusion.

jokes.text: Also, it turns out that stealing the Queen Mary comes under maritime law, which, I just found out, is an actual thing. It's a real thing.
jokes.setup: Barry is explaining the legal complications
jokes.punchline: He's surprised that maritime law exists at all
jokes.explanation: Great Barry moment - his surprise that a major area of law exists shows his complete incompetence. The repetition 'It's a real thing' sells it.

jokes.text: I also lost a perfectly good pair of human chest hair nipple tufts when we hit the water.
jokes.setup: Stan is explaining his losses from the Queen Mary incident
jokes.punchline: He mourns his fake chest hair with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Hilariously specific and pathetic detail. Perfect for Stan's character and the phrase 'human chest hair nipple tufts' is memorably grotesque.

jokes.text: That's kelp; why would I put it onto my chest or head or anywhere else? I was gonna show those babies off at the Jacuzzi after-party.
jokes.setup: Stan is explaining his fake hair situation
jokes.punchline: He was planning to show off his fake chest hair at a party
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of Stan's pathetic vanity. The image of him showing off fake chest hair at a party is appropriately cringe-worthy.

jokes.text: Well, maybe not Halliburton, but definitely Halliburton Teen.
jokes.setup: Stan is bragging about the size of his potential business
jokes.punchline: He compares himself to 'Halliburton Teen,' a nonsensical youth brand version of the defense contractor
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd concept - a teen lifestyle brand version of a controversial defense contractor. Very original and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Halliburton Teen was a leading youth and lifestyle retailer, despite the fact that it was a rebranding of the tarnished Halliburton Penitentiary and Rendition Systems.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Halliburton Teen
jokes.punchline: It's a rebranding of a prison and torture company into a teen retailer
jokes.explanation: Extremely dark and original concept. The casual tone describing prison/torture company → teen retailer pipeline is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Oh... aah...! Ow, my hair! My beautiful hair! My hair! My beautiful hair!
jokes.setup: Stan is caught looking at secret plans
jokes.punchline: He cries about his 'beautiful hair' being pulled when he's completely bald
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy callback to Stan's alopecia. The irony of lamenting beautiful hair when bald works well for his character's vanity.

jokes.text: This isn't what it looks like! Oh, really? Because it looks like a monument to George W. Bush. Well, you got me.
jokes.setup: Stan tries to deny what George Sr. is seeing
jokes.punchline: He immediately gives up when George Sr. correctly identifies it
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection where denial immediately crumbles. Good economy but not particularly original or memorable.

jokes.text: It'll never stand. It's too thin. It'll never stand. You see, the W's and the word 'bush.' I mean, what else could it be?
jokes.setup: George Sr. criticizes the monument's structural integrity
jokes.punchline: He's looking at architectural plans sideways and doesn't realize it's a wall
jokes.explanation: Good visual misdirection joke. The reveal that he's looking at it wrong creates a nice 'aha' moment. Benefits from rewatching.

jokes.text: It's a wall, George; you're looking at it sideways. The W stands for 'wall.' The bushes... Stand for the bushes.
jokes.setup: Stan reveals George Sr. misunderstood the plans
jokes.punchline: His explanation is obvious and redundant - bushes stand for bushes
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the misdirection setup. The deadpan redundancy of 'bushes stand for bushes' works as anti-climactic explanation.

jokes.text: Ah--this explains the immigration booth 10,000 feet in the air.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is now understanding the wall plans correctly
jokes.punchline: He notices an absurd detail - an immigration booth impossibly high in the air
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist observation. The image of an immigration booth miles in the air is perfectly ridiculous and well-delivered.

jokes.text: You said that right after Lupe got the bleach stain on your teal blazer.
jokes.setup: Stan is referencing when George Sr. came up with the anti-immigration idea
jokes.punchline: It was motivated by a domestic accident with his housekeeper
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing George Sr.'s petty motivations for major policy positions. The specificity of 'teal blazer' sells it.

jokes.text: Bluth for two--I mean, one.
jokes.setup: George Sr. making restaurant reservation
jokes.punchline: Accidentally revealing he expected company but is alone
jokes.explanation: Small moment showing George Sr.'s loneliness and social awkwardness

jokes.text: I thought I already seated you.
jokes.setup: Host confused about seating George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Implying he looks identical to his twin Oscar
jokes.explanation: Sets up twin confusion running gag throughout episode

jokes.text: George! Actually, we came in to use the bathroom, but they sat me. And they have mahimahi today. Will you join us? Will you have a mahimahi on me? On you.
jokes.setup: Oscar invites George Sr. to join them for lunch
jokes.punchline: He makes an unintentional sexual innuendo with 'mahimahi on me' then awkwardly tries to clarify
jokes.explanation: Mild sexual innuendo joke that fits Oscar's character. Not particularly original but the awkward clarification works.

jokes.text: This is Heartfire; she's an aura specialist. She did Matthew Modine. I will.
jokes.setup: Oscar is introducing Heartfire's professional credentials
jokes.punchline: The phrasing suggests sexual services, and Heartfire confirms interest
jokes.explanation: Simple double entendre that works but isn't particularly clever. Gets points for economy but relies on easy sexual misunderstanding.

jokes.text: The bigger crime would've been the patient didn't wake up and testify against me. But nobody cares about the part of the oath he kept.
jokes.setup: Dr. Norman is explaining his medical malpractice situation
jokes.punchline: He suggests it would have been worse if he'd killed the patient, then claims moral credit for not being a murderer
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that works well. The twisted logic of taking credit for not committing murder is appropriately disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: If he as bad as the state of California say he is, then why did I fall in love with him, huh? Yo, [bleep] the state of California! [bleep] 'em! Bunch of California [bleep], yeah.
jokes.setup: China Garden is defending Dr. Norman against the state's charges
jokes.punchline: Her defense immediately escalates to profane attacks on California
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from romantic defense to random state-bashing. The character's aggressive loyalty is well-established and the profanity escalation works.

jokes.text: Oh, great. Now you're gonna make me cry. Oh, lizard! Look! Look, a lizard! Cute!
jokes.setup: China Garden getting emotional about being kicked off land
jokes.punchline: Immediately distracted by random lizard
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing scattered attention span and emotional volatility

jokes.text: We're getting a divorce. I want to know about the stimulus package. We haven't had sex since Christmas, Michael!
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille are pretending to fight about divorce
jokes.punchline: They overshare intimate details while Michael is present
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy with appropriate TMI factor. The parents oversharing in front of their son creates effective discomfort.

jokes.text: You had sex seven weeks ago? They were remodeling my room, and I pulled my cot in there, just like camp. We yelled at you to leave. Yeah, but then you whispered, 'Don't pull out!'
jokes.setup: Michael is horrified to learn his parents had recent sex
jokes.punchline: The situation gets worse when he reveals he was present and heard intimate details
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of cringe factor. The progression from Michael learning about the sex to revealing he was present to the 'don't pull out' detail is perfectly uncomfortable.

jokes.text: You had sex seven weeks ago?
jokes.setup: Lucille saying they haven't had sex since Christmas
jokes.punchline: Michael calculating timeline and being disturbed by recent activity
jokes.explanation: Perfect son's horrified reaction to parents' sex life revelation

jokes.text: I'm John Beard, and I'm a Gloomy Gus. I don't have a mustache.
jokes.setup: John Beard is doing his news broadcast
jokes.punchline: He makes self-deprecating comments about his personality and appearance
jokes.explanation: Mild meta joke about the character breaking his professional demeanor. Works for the character but not particularly strong.

jokes.text: having found a way around both the building's strict no-smoking policy... and the fact that her ankle monitor prevented her from approaching the balcony.
jokes.setup: Lucille wants to smoke but faces two obstacles
jokes.punchline: She's found a creative solution (implied to be smoking indoors illegally)
jokes.explanation: Good setup for visual gag showing Lucille's determination to smoke despite multiple legal obstacles. Shows character resourcefulness in wrong ways.

jokes.text: I have to stop. I have to stop. So... But, finally, I just ordered... pastrami shortcake, like I said, but I didn't order... I didn't order sprinkles this-- Oh, Mother, please. Please.
jokes.setup: Lucille is rambling incoherently during a video call
jokes.punchline: Her story makes no sense and Michael is exasperated
jokes.explanation: Rambling nonsense that shows Lucille's state but isn't particularly funny. The 'pastrami shortcake' detail is absurd but the execution is scattered.

jokes.text: I'd give $10,000 for a glass of lemonade right now. Sweat... and squeeze.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is suffering in his own sweat lodge
jokes.punchline: He desperately wants the same lemonade he was pouring out earlier, leading to his business epiphany
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that shows karma and leads to his business model revelation. The irony of desperately wanting what he wasted is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Dr. Norman, we have a hot mess. George Sr. had been passed out for two days after having a vision...
jokes.setup: Someone calls for Dr. Norman about George Sr.'s condition
jokes.punchline: 'Hot mess' is literal (he's overheated) and figurative (his situation is chaotic)
jokes.explanation: Decent pun that works on multiple levels but not particularly memorable. Good economy but straightforward execution.

jokes.text: Does it come with lemonade? It comes with all the lemonade you can drink. 'Squeeze' had two meanings.
jokes.setup: A CEO asks about lemonade with the expensive program
jokes.punchline: George Sr. promises unlimited lemonade while the narrator reveals 'squeeze' means extortion
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the lemonade motif and clever double meaning revelation. The structure works well to reveal the scam nature.

jokes.text: Does it come with lemonade? It comes with all the lemonade you can drink.
jokes.setup: CEO asking about what's included in expensive package
jokes.punchline: George Sr. promising unlimited lemonade as if it's valuable
jokes.explanation: Callback to lemonade theme while showing the scam's absurd value proposition

jokes.text: Hey. Hey. He had his twin brother endure it. Did you bring me some lemonade? No, sir. It is crazy out there. For the first time we are out.
jokes.setup: George Sr. appears resilient in the sweat lodge
jokes.punchline: Revealed that Oscar is doing the suffering while George Sr. stays cool
jokes.explanation: Excellent reveal that recontextualizes George Sr.'s apparent superhuman heat tolerance. Great character integration showing his exploitation of Oscar.

jokes.text: My wife doesn't understand me. My angel. I haven't had an orgasm outside of my bathtub in 30 years. How's that? Although, at times, I do wish it were darker.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille are roleplaying adultery scenarios
jokes.punchline: Lucille's oversharing becomes uncomfortably specific, then she critiques the lighting
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe comedy showing the couple's disturbing relationship dynamics. Lucille's matter-of-fact delivery of TMI is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I don't know what's going on. For some reason I'm just getting Richard Burton today. Oh, my God, what's that? That's the commode.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is having maca-induced visions
jokes.punchline: Instead of Elizabeth Taylor, he sees Richard Burton, then notices the toilet
jokes.explanation: Decent continuation of the vision gag with less appealing results. The commode detail adds grossness but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: You trespass on sacred ground. The spirits command me to warn you. You must leave this land or the land will change you. Don't worry, he's probably from a local reservation. I'll, I'll take care of this.
jokes.setup: A mystical warning appears to George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. dismisses spiritual prophecy as a casino marketing opportunity
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George Sr.'s materialistic worldview. His immediate pivot to casino booking shows his priorities.

jokes.text: You trespass on sacred ground. The spirits command me to warn you. You must leave this land or the land will change you.
jokes.setup: Mysterious figure appearing to deliver ominous warning
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top mystical dialogue delivered by guy in ostrich costume
jokes.explanation: Absurd mystical warning that sets up prophecy subplot

jokes.text: How about you book me two nights for Ray Romano at your casino?
jokes.setup: George Sr. responding to mystical warning from ostrich guy
jokes.punchline: Treating spiritual encounter as casino booking opportunity
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing George Sr.'s capitalist mindset overwhelming everything

jokes.text: How about you book me two nights for Ray Romano at your casino? The strong will become the weak, the weak will become the strong. And get us close to the front but not close enough that Ray talks to us.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is negotiating entertainment bookings during a spiritual warning
jokes.punchline: He has specific seating preferences for Ray Romano while ignoring prophecy
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor mixing mystical prophecy with mundane celebrity preferences. The specificity of not wanting Ray to talk to them is perfect.

jokes.text: I don't know what you saw, but I got an ostrich and no boner.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille had different maca-induced visions
jokes.punchline: George Sr. matter-of-factly reports his disappointing vision results
jokes.explanation: Hilariously blunt and unexpected line. The combination of mystical disappointment and sexual frustration delivered deadpan is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: I don't know what you saw, but I got an ostrich and no boner.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille comparing their maca experiences
jokes.punchline: Crude summary of his disappointing hallucination experience
jokes.explanation: Perfectly crude and specific summary that's shockingly honest for George Sr.

jokes.text: Come on, guys, I looked the other way on the bottled water because you look thirsty, but you got to play along here. Heartfire? Where's the lemonade? Shh!
jokes.setup: George Sr. is losing control of his audience
jokes.punchline: Even his silent assistant is questioning him, then shushes him
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of George Sr.'s declining authority. Heartfire breaking her silence gimmick to question him, then shushing him, works well.

jokes.text: No, have you seen China Garden? No. Did you kill that armadillo? No, no, he's just under.
jokes.setup: George Sr. looking for Oscar, asking Dr. Norman for help
jokes.punchline: Casual discussion about potentially dead armadillo and missing person
jokes.explanation: Darkly absurd casual conversation about potentially serious situations

jokes.text: Did you kill that armadillo? No, no, he's just under. If only we could use some of his medicine for our own pain. Oh, wait, I've got a full syringe in the car.
jokes.setup: Someone asks about a dead armadillo
jokes.punchline: Dr. Norman offers to get anesthesia from his car to drug the animal
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that fits Dr. Norman's character as disgraced anesthesiologist. The casual offer to drug an armadillo is appropriately disturbing.

jokes.text: Wait, wait, that rally is tonight? Mm-hmm. But I'm supposed to meet my wife tonight, which I am dreading, by the way.
jokes.setup: George Sr. has a scheduling conflict
jokes.punchline: He admits he's dreading meeting his wife despite their romantic roleplay
jokes.explanation: Good character honesty that undercuts the romantic pretense. Shows the reality beneath their performance.

jokes.text: You're so ugly from behind.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille are being intimate
jokes.punchline: Lucille delivers a brutal insult during sex
jokes.explanation: Perfectly brutal Lucille line with maximum economy. The timing and cruelty are spot-on for her character.

jokes.text: Too bad you can't use a double. Oh, also I used a look-alike named Stewart to take the California bar for me. But you knew that, right?
jokes.setup: Barry suggests using a double, then admits professional fraud
jokes.punchline: He casually reveals he cheated on his bar exam with a look-alike
jokes.explanation: Excellent Barry moment revealing his complete professional fraud. The casual admission and 'but you knew that, right?' sells his obliviousness.

jokes.text: I just can't go on pretending to be you, sitting silently... Shh. ...in a, in a 100-degree mud hut for an hour and a half.
jokes.setup: Oscar is complaining about having to be silent
jokes.punchline: George Sr. shushes him while he's explaining why he can't be silent
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment where George Sr. demonstrates exactly the behavior Oscar is complaining about. Well-timed interruption.

jokes.text: I just pooped in a hole and covered it up. I hope I did the right thing. There's a commode. It's, it's directly uphill from the maca root.
jokes.setup: Oscar reports his bathroom activities to Barry
jokes.punchline: Barry reveals this contaminated their drug supply
jokes.explanation: Payoff to earlier setup about contamination. Gross-out humor that works but isn't particularly sophisticated.

jokes.text: I love it when you call my name. George! Don't call me that. Call me Father B.
jokes.setup: Lucille is being romantic with who she thinks is George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Oscar breaks character by objecting to being called George, then makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Good ending to the identity confusion. Oscar's terrible impersonation skills create a fitting conclusion to his discovery.

tmdb_overview: George Sr. comes up with a get rich quick scam after his family falls apart.
insight: Lindsay's narcissism drives 18 of 58 jokes, anchoring the episode's cringe-comedy backbone.
